content.text: Microsoft reports that MFA blocks over 99.9% of automated account compromise attacks. Password-only authentication is vulnerable to phishing, credential stuffing, and spray attacks. Adding a second factor means an attacker who obtains a user's password still cannot sign in without the second factor.

In Entra ID, MFA is enforced through Conditional Access policies that require one or more factors beyond the password. The Authentication Methods Policy controls which methods users can register and use tenant-wide.

attrs.alt: MFA methods comparison — phishing resistance and authentication strength

content.text: Entra ID supports a spectrum of second-factor methods, ordered from most to least phishing-resistant. The right method for a user population depends on the threat model, device capabilities, and regulatory requirements.

* Push notifications with number matching are not phishing-resistant but are resistant to MFA fatigue attacks (the main attack against simple push notifications). For truly phishing-resistant MFA, use FIDO2, WHfB, or CBA.

The Authentication Methods Policy is the canonical place to configure which MFA methods are enabled, for which users or AUs, and with which feature settings. The legacy per-user MFA portal (manage.windowsazure.com) is deprecated — use the Graph API or Entra admin center instead.

MFA enforcement belongs in Conditional Access, not in individual method settings. A CA policy grants access only when MFA has been performed. You can require any MFA method (the 'mfa' built-in control) or require a specific Authentication Strength tier.
Privileged roles, high-value targets, NIST AAL3
Authenticator push/TOTP, SMS, voice, FIDO2, WHfB, CBA

Before enforcing MFA via Conditional Access, users must register at least one MFA method. A registration campaign interrupts sign-in to prompt unregistered users, with a configurable snooze period. This is gentler than hard-blocking unregistered users.

When a user receives an unexpected MFA push and taps 'Report fraud' (or enters the fraud code), Entra ID logs a fraud alert and can optionally block the user. These alerts signal that an attacker has the user's password and is attempting sign-in. Fraud alerts should trigger an immediate investigation.

# Monitor MFA fraud alerts and suspicious push notifications
# When number matching is enabled, MFA fatigue attacks are largely defeated
# But fraud alerts still surface real attack attempts

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

# MFA fraud alerts appear as risk events in Identity Protection
# and as audit events in directory audit logs

# Option 1: Check for fraud alerts in audit logs
resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/directoryAudits"
    "?$filter=category eq 'UserManagement' and activityDisplayName eq 'Fraud reported - user is blocked for MFA'"
    "&$top=50&$orderby=activityDateTime desc",
    headers=headers
)
alerts = resp.json().get("value", [])
print(f"Fraud alerts in audit logs: {len(alerts)}")
for a in alerts:
    targets = [t.get("userPrincipalName","?") for t in a.get("targetResources",[])]
    print(f"  {a['activityDateTime'][:19]}  Targets: {', '.join(targets)}")

# Option 2: Query risky sign-ins related to MFA anomaly
risky_resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskDetections"
    "?$filter=riskEventType eq 'suspiciousInboxManipulationRules' or "
    "riskEventType eq 'unfamiliarFeatures'"
    "&$select=userPrincipalName,riskEventType,riskLevel,detectedDateTime"
    "&$top=20",
    headers=headers
)
for det in risky_resp.json().get("value", []):
    print(f"  {det['detectedDateTime'][:10]}  {det['userPrincipalName']}  {det['riskEventType']}  level={det['riskLevel']}")

# Option 3: Automated response — block user when fraud alert fires
def block_user_on_fraud(token, upn):
    requests.patch(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{upn}",
        headers={**headers, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={"accountEnabled": False}
    )
    print(f"Blocked {upn} after fraud alert")

Conditional Access exclusions — break-glass accounts, service principals, or temporary exemptions — must be tracked. Over time, exclusion lists grow and become security debt. Regularly audit which users and groups are excluded from MFA policies and verify each exclusion is justified.

Administrators can view and revoke a user's registered FIDO2 security keys via Microsoft Graph. This is useful when a user reports a lost or stolen key, or when offboarding an employee. Most users register their own keys at aka.ms/mysecurityinfo — the programmatic API is primarily for admin management and auditing.

Before enforcing MFA via Conditional Access, you need to know what fraction of your user population has registered a qualifying method. The User Registration Details report in Microsoft Graph provides per-user registration state, including which methods are registered and whether the user is considered 'MFA capable'.

# Read combined registration details for all users
# Shows: isMfaCapable, isMfaRegistered, methods registered, sspr-capable

import requests

def get_registration_report(token):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    results = []
    url = ("https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/reports/authenticationMethods/userRegistrationDetails"
           "?$select=userPrincipalName,isMfaCapable,isMfaRegistered,isSsprCapable,"
           "isSsprRegistered,methodsRegistered&$top=999")
    
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
        data = resp.json()
        results.extend(data.get("value", []))
        url = data.get("@odata.nextLink")
    
    # Categorise
    strong = [u for u in results if any(m in u.get("methodsRegistered", [])
              for m in ["fido2", "windowsHelloForBusiness", "microsoftAuthenticatorPush"])]
    weak_only = [u for u in results if u.get("isMfaRegistered") and u not in strong]
    none_reg = [u for u in results if not u.get("isMfaRegistered")]
    
    print(f"Total users:              {len(results)}")
    print(f"  Strong MFA (FIDO2/WHfB/push): {len(strong)}")
    print(f"  Weak MFA only (SMS/TOTP):     {len(weak_only)}")
    print(f"  No MFA registered:            {len(none_reg)}")
    
    if none_reg:
        print(f"\nTop 10 unregistered:")
        for u in none_reg[:10]:
            print(f"  {u['userPrincipalName']}")
    
    return results

token = get_token()
get_registration_report(token)
Access granted; consider migrating to stronger method
CA blocks access or redirects to registration
CA blocks access; registration campaign needed first

Conditional Access can exempt trusted network locations (office IP ranges, Azure IP ranges) from MFA requirements. However, bypassing MFA for trusted locations is a security trade-off: if an attacker gains access to the trusted network, they bypass MFA entirely. For high-security environments, require MFA everywhere and use passwordless methods to reduce friction instead.

# Define named locations and use them in CA policies
# Trusted locations can skip MFA (e.g. office IP ranges)
# — use with caution; prefer requiring MFA everywhere

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# 1. Create a named IP location (office IP ranges)
ip_location = {
    "@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.ipNamedLocation",
    "displayName": "Contoso Office IPs",
    "isTrusted": True,     # marks as trusted for CA evaluation
    "ipRanges": [
        {"@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.iPv4CidrRange", "cidrAddress": "203.0.113.0/24"},
        {"@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.iPv4CidrRange", "cidrAddress": "198.51.100.0/24"},
    ]
}
resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/namedLocations",
    headers=headers,
    json=ip_location
)
office_loc_id = resp.json().get("id")
print(f"Created location: {office_loc_id}")

# 2. Create a CA policy that skips MFA from trusted office IPs
# (Alternative: use Identity Protection sign-in risk instead)
ca_skip_mfa_office = {
    "displayName": "Skip MFA from Trusted Office IPs",
    "state": "enabled",
    "conditions": {
        "users": {"includeUsers": ["All"], "excludeGroups": ["BREAK-GLASS-ID"]},
        "applications": {"includeApplications": ["All"]},
        "locations": {
            "includeLocations": ["All"],
            "excludeLocations": [office_loc_id]   # CA doesn't apply when at office
        }
    },
    "grantControls": {
        "operator": "OR",
        "builtInControls": ["mfa"]
    }
}
# Note: Named locations as MFA bypass are discouraged for high-security environments
# because office IP trust can be bypassed via VPN or compromised network equipment.
# Consider requiring MFA everywhere and using passwordless methods instead.
MFA Session Lifetime and Continuous Access Evaluation

By default, MFA tokens have a long lifetime. Conditional Access sign-in frequency controls force re-authentication after a configurable period. Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) complements this by allowing Entra ID to revoke long-lived tokens in near-real-time when account state changes — no need to wait for token expiry.
Sign-in frequency: 1 hour for privileged roles, 24 hours for normal workforce, 7 days for low-risk read-only apps
Persistent browser session 'never' + sign-in frequency together give predictable re-auth behaviour
CAE is automatic for Exchange Online and SharePoint Online — no configuration needed
CAE-enabled apps receive token revocation within seconds of account disablement or password change

MFA in Entra ID is configured through two complementary systems: the Authentication Methods Policy (controls which methods are available) and Conditional Access (enforces which methods are required for which scenarios). For privileged roles, require phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2, WHfB, or CBA) via an Authentication Strength policy. For the general workforce, require multifactor auth with number matching enabled on push notifications to defeat MFA fatigue attacks. Monitor registration rates, fraud alerts, and CA exclusions regularly.
FIDO2, Windows Hello for Business, and Certificate-Based Auth are the only phishing-resistant MFA methods in Entra ID
Enable number matching on Authenticator push — it was made mandatory by Microsoft in 2023
Enforce MFA through Conditional Access policies, not per-user MFA settings (deprecated)
Run a registration campaign before enabling enforcement to avoid locking out unregistered users
Fraud alerts signal a real attack — treat them as security incidents requiring password reset and session revocation
Audit CA exclusions quarterly — temporary exemptions become permanent without review
